rulings.Case Title: OREGON MUTUAL INSURANCE COMPANY VS. BRUCE I. GILBERT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Sunsets Further Responses To Special Interrogatories; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Brian J. Ferber
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 27, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF OREGON MUTUAL INSURANCE COMPANY'S Motion To Compel Sunsets Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Oregon Mutual Insurance Co.'s ("Oregon Mutual's") Motion to Compel Sunset's Further Responses to Special Interrogatories is DENIED. Defendant Sunset Scavenger Co. ("Sunset") declined to request monetary sanctions and for that reason monetary sanctions are DENIED AS UNSOUGHT. All other sanctions, if any, are DENIED. Sunset shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. FACTS A fire occurred at 3312-3316 Mission Street in San Francisco on June 18, 2016. Oregon Mutual alleges liability against Sunset and others for harm caused by the fire. PROCEDURAL HISTORY On May 9, 2019, Oregon Mutual propounded 1335 special interrogatories on Sunset. Sunset timely responded on July 12, 2019. The Parties subsequently met and conferred as to the adequacy of Sunset's responses. DISCUSSION The 45 Day Rule. It is a basic tenet of the Discovery Act that in order to compel further responses to interrogatories, the movant must notice the respective motions "within 45 days of the service of the verified response, or any supplemental verified response, or on or before any specific later date to which the propounding party and the responding party have agreed in writing." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.300(c). Failure to timely notice said motions results in waiver. Id.; see Sexton v. Superior Court, 58 Cal. App. 4th 1403, 1410 (1997) (holding "[f]ailure to timely move to compel . . . constitutes a waiver of any right to compel a further response; indeed, similar provisions have been held at least quasi-jurisdictional . . .[in] the sense that it renders the court without authority to rule on motions to compel other than to deny them"). Here, Sunset responded to Oregon Mutual's 1335 special interrogatories on July 12, 2019. The forty-five days would have run on August 26, 2019. But a few days prior, on September 23, 2019, the Parties agreed in written e-mail to extend the due date to file a motion to compel further responses to October 17, 2019. That date came and went. The Instant Motion was filed on November 1, 2019, fifteen days later. By then the time had expired to bring this Motion. It is time barred. (end of tenative ruling Part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: OREGON MUTUAL INSURANCE COMPANY VS. BRUCE I. GILBERT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Oregon Mutual Insurance Company'S Motion To Compel Sunsets Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 27, 2019, Line 1, Plaintiff Oregon Mutual Insurance Company'S Motion To Compel Sunsets Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 2 Of 2) Oregon Mutual argues that it had an oral agreement with Sunset's counsel for an "open extension to file motions." but the Code does not permit oral agreements for extensions of time to the 45 day rule. It requires any such agreement to be "in writing." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.300(c). Oregon Mutual argues that it was for Sunset to "say something" in response to Oregon Mutual's unilateral e-mails related to further extensions. But under the Code, one party's silence is not sufficient to extend the deadline. To the contrary, in the absence of mutual written consent, it was beholden upon Oregon Mutual to assume that the time to bring a discovery motion was the date set by the Parties in writing. That date was October 17, 2019. The Instant Motion is time barred. Ancillary Issues. The Court notes the following ancillary issues-none of which are the basis for the Court's denial of the Motion. First, Oregon Mutual's separate statement is inadequate. See Cal. R. Ct. Rule 3.1345(a). While the separate statement refers to over a thousand special interrogatories, it recapitulates but one. Second, Oregon Mutual failed to provide to this Court a full copy of the Special Interrogatories (or Responses) offering only the first three dozen of the 1335. Without the full discovery, this Court lacks a basis to grant the Instant Motion, even if it were not time barred. Oregon Mutual complains that it could not submit the entire discovery to the Court because to do so would "take at least 1500 pages" and the "Court would not approve of such a motion." This may be true but this is problem of Oregon Mutual's own making; and it highlights the final, more fundamental issue: Third, propounding 1335 Special Interrogatories is presumptively excessive. The volume of this written discovery is a burden to propound, a burden to respond to, and a burden for this Court to adjudicate. This is why parties are presumptively limited to 35. See Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2030.030(a)(1). The Court appreciates that litigants can get around the Rule of 35 with a declaration of necessity but just because parties can propound excessive written discovery does not mean they should. Related Joinders. Other parties, WESTCHESTER SURPLUS LINES INSURANCE COMPANY, and FARMERS INSURANCE EXCHANGE, have joined the Instant Motion. Those Joinders are DENIED upon the same basis and for the same reasons as stated above. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tenative ruling part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Deposition Of Mohammaed Khan And Production Of Documents And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, November 27, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF SAN FRANCISCO INDEPENDENT TAX ASSOCIATION'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Mohammaed Khan And Production Of Documents And Request For Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. rulings.Case Title: CHARLOTTE KERNAN ET AL VS. REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or Alternatively , Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, November 27, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANTS REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, ELEANOR DREY, M.D., AND WILLIAM WINKELMAN, M.D.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication. Defendants' motion for summary judgment/adjudication is ordered off calendar until such time as they produce - and plaintiffs have an opportunity to review - the documents sought by plaintiffs' October 16, 2019 requests. (CCP Sec. 473c(h)("or make any other order as may be just").) Defendants deride plaintiffs' document requests as "boilerplate," but I find them substantive. Defendants also criticize procedures the pro per plaintiffs used to invoke Sec. 473c(h), but the document requests are attached to a declaration and the requests' import is plain both from their face and from plaintiffs' other papers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MERCURY INSURANCE COMPANY VS. MUSONDA KABWE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Tovacate Default And Default Judgment, If Entered; Points And Authorities; Declaration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, November 27, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT MUSONDA KABWE'S Motion To Vacate Default And Default Judgment, If Entered. Defendant Musonda Kabwe's "motion to vacate default and default judgment if entered" is denied without prejudice to refiling. CCP Sec. 473(b) requires that such an application for relief "shall be accompanied by a copy of the answer or other pleading proposed to be filed therein, otherwise the application shall not be granted." Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: NORTHERN CALIFORNIA COLLECTION SERVICE, INC. VS. NU CONCEPTS MANAGEMENT GROUP INC. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, November 27, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA COLLECTION SERVICE, INC.'S HEARING ON CLAIM OF EXEMPTION. Defendant Nu Concept Management Group, Inc.'s claim of exemption is denied. First, only a "natural person" can claim an exemption and defendant is a corporation. (See CCP Sec. 703.020(a).) Second, the paid earnings exemption of CCP Sec. 704.070 applies to payees. Defendant is attempting to invoke the exemption as a payor. Any money that the sheriff is holding shall be released to the judgment creditor. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/RBU)
